# 6 Biotransformation of Xenobiotics
# Casarett & Doull's Toxicology, 9th Edition
# Pages: 212-419
# Unit: Unit II Disposition of Toxicants

6
chapter
Biotransformation of Xenobiotics
Andrew Parkinson, Brian W. Ogilvie, David B. Buckley, 
Faraz Kazmi and Oliver Parkinson
INTRODUCTION
The overall fate or disposition of a xenobiotic, which encom-
passes its absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination 
or ADME (an acronym commonly used by the pharmaceutical 
industry), is determined by three major factors, namely passive 
diffusion across biological membranes or between cells (i.e., 
transcellular and paracellular passive diffusion, respectively), 
facilitated transport by uptake and/or efflux transporters, and 
biotransformation (invariably called metabolism in the case of 
drugs). In the case of xenobiotics that permeate cells with a high 
rate of passive diffusion, which enables them to be absorbed from 
the gastrointestinal tract, lung, or skin (depending on the route 
of exposure), biotransformation plays a key role in their elimina-
tion. Without biotransformation, a highly permeable xenobiotic 
excreted in urine or bile will be reabsorbed in the kidney or intes-
tine rather than eliminated in urine or feces. If a xenobiotic can 
permeate cells with a high rate of passive diffusion but it cannot 
be biotransformed at an appreciable rate then the xenobiotic can 
have a long residency time in the body. Dioxin (2,3,7,8-tetrachol-
orodibenzo-p-dioxin or TCDD) illustrates this principle. Dioxin

permeates cells with a high rate of passive diffusion, but it is 
largely resistant to biotransformation. For this reason, dioxin and 
numerous other polyhalogenated aromatic hydrocarbons (PCDFs, 
PCBs, PBBs, etc.), industrial chemicals present in the environ-
ment, are renowned for their persistence in the body and for their 
bioaccumulation in the food chain. The lesson from dioxin and 
related compounds, namely that halogenation is not an obstacle to 
passive permeation but is an obstacle to biotransformation, is not 
lost on the pharmaceutical industry. Many drugs are fluorinated 
(and some are chlorinated, brominated or iodinated) at meta-
bolically labile sites to slow their rate of biotransformation and 
thereby prolong their residency time in the body in order to extend 
their therapeutic effect, which may make once-a-day dosing at a 
low dose feasible (Obach et al., 2016).
In all previous versions of this chapter, biotransformation has 
been described as an enzymatic process of chemical modification 
that changes the physicochemical properties of a xenobiotic from 
those that favor absorption and distribution (i.e., high passive per-
meability associated with high lipophilicity) to those that favor 
elimination (i.e., low passive permeability associated with high 
hydrophilicity). This rationalization of biotransformation is still

194
Disposition of toxicants
unit II
valid but it overlooks an important principle, namely that facilitated 
transport also plays a key role in the disposition of xenobiotics, 
including those whose elimination is determined by their rate of 
biotransformation. Facilitated transport resolves a conundrum that 
has long been overlooked in drug metabolism circles: If biotransfor-
mation converts a lipophilic xenobiotic to hydrophilic metabolites, 
how do the hydrophilic metabolites get out of the cell that forms 
them? The short answer is: They are transported out of the cell. In 
many cases transporters play an important role in the elimination 
of drugs whose systemic clearance is primarily determined by their 
rate of biotransformation in the liver. The opposite is also true: In 
many cases biotransformation plays an important role in the elimi-
nation of drugs whose systemic clearance is primarily determined 
by their rate of transporter-mediated uptake into liver. Whether 
biotransformation (with support from transporters) or facilitated 
transport (with support from biotransformation) plays the key role 
in the elimination of a drug depends on the physicochemical proper-
ties of the xenobiotic. The following information on the importance 
of physicochemical properties comes largely from studies of drug 
disposition, but the principles apply to all xenobiotics.
Charge is an obstacle to membrane permeation by passive dif-
fusion. There are several drugs that resemble the neurotransmitter 
acetylcholine, CH3CO‒O‒C2H4‒N+(CH3)3, inasmuch as they con-
tain one or more quaternary ammonium ions (R1,R2,R3,R4N+) and 
are used to block neurotransmission by acetylcholine (nicotinic or 
muscarinic acetylcholine receptor antagonists such as alcuronium, 
atracurium, cisatracurium, doxacurium, metocurine, mivacurium, 
pancuronium, rocuronium, tubocurarine, and vecuronium) or inhibit 
the hydrolysis of acetylcholine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitors such 
as edrophonium, neostigmine, and pyridostigmine). All of these 
drugs have extracellular targets (acetylcholinesterase in the neuro-
muscular gap junction or acetylcholine receptors on synaptic cell 
surfaces) and they are all administered by intramuscular (IM) or 
intravenous (IV) injection because their permanent positive charge 
(due to the quaternary ammonium ion) is an obstacle to oral absorp-
tion from the gastrointestinal tract. (Pyridostigmine is administered 
orally but at 30 times the IV dose.) Xenobiotics with a permanent 
positive charge do not permeate membranes with a high rate of pas-
sive diffusion but they can enter cells if they are substrates for an 
uptake transporter. Because of its permanent positive charge, tetra-
methylammonium ion, (CH3)4N+, does not permeate membranes 
with a high rate of passive diffusion; however, it is transported into 
hepatocytes and is used in vitro as a probe/index substrate for hepatic 
organic cation transporter-1 (OCT1).1 Metformin, a natural product 
used to treat type 2 diabetes, is widely used as an in vitro probe/index 
substrate for OCT2, a renal organic cation transporter. Metformin 
does not have a single permanent positive charge, but it does have a 
pair of basic nitrogen atoms with high pKa(B) values (10.3 and 12.3), 
each of which is >99.9% ionized at pH 7.4, which results in a minis-
cule percentage of drug in the nonionized state in blood.
Drugs with a permanent charge are the minority. The majority 
of drugs contain one or more ionizable groups, such as a carbox-
ylic acid moiety (R–COOH ⇌ R–COO− + H+) or a basic amine 
(R–NH3
+ ⇌ R–NH2 + H+). Only the nonionized form of these 
drugs permeates cell membranes with a high rate of passive 
diffusion. The ionized and nonionized (neutral) forms are in pH-
dependent equilibrium. The pH producing equal amounts of the ion-
ized and nonionized form of an ionizable group is defined as pKa(A) 
in the case of acids and pKa(B) in the case of bases. Physiological 
pH values range widely: pH 1.5 to 3 in the fasted stomach, pH 3 to 
5 in the fed stomach, pH 5 to 8 in the small intestine, pH 4 to 5 in 
lysosomes, pH 5 to 6 in endosomes, pH 7.2 in cytosol, and pH 7.4 
in blood (Newton and Kluza, 1978). Consequently, for compounds 
with ionizable groups, the ratio of ionized-to-neutral drug varies 
throughout the body. Studies of drug permeation across artificial 
phospholipid membranes (the parallel artificial membrane perme-
ation assay or PAMPA), which are devoid of transporters, reaffirm 
the lesson from permanently charged drugs, namely, that perme-
ation of membranes with a high rate of passive diffusion is a prop-
erty of the nonionized form of a drug, which is favored by low pH 
in the case of acidic drugs and high pH in the case of basic drugs 
(Ruell et al., 2003).
In addition to charge, two other physicochemical proper-
ties impact the permeation of drugs by passive diffusion, namely 
log P and topological polar surface area (TPSA). log P is a mea-
sure of the lipophilicity of the nonionized form of a drug. log P 
is the octanol–water partition ratio (the ratio of the concentration 
of nonionized drug in octanol vs. the concentration of nonionized 
drug in water) expressed on a log10 scale. Incidentally, log D, the 
distribution coefficient, takes into account the concentration of ion-
ized drug in water, which is pH dependent; hence, log D values 
for compounds with ionizable groups vary with pH, whereas log P, 
which applies only to the nonionized drug, is a constant. Permeation 
of membranes by passive diffusion increases with increasing log P. 
However, increasing log P can come at the expense of aqueous solu-
bility; hence, drugs with a log P >5 often show poor oral bioavail-
ability because they do not dissolve in gastrointestinal fluid. This 
consideration generally has limited applicability to xenobiotics like 
dioxin where prolonged exposure to low concentrations, rather than 
once-a-day dosing to a drug at high concentrations, is the norm.
TPSA is an overall measure of the number of oxygen and 
nitrogen atoms that bind water by functioning as hydrogen donors 
or hydrogen acceptors. For example, dimethylamine, (CH3)2NH, 
has one hydrogen donor that can form a hydrogen bond with the 
oxygen atom of water, (CH3)2NH ||||| OH2, and one hydrogen 
acceptor (the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen) that can form 
a hydrogen bond with a hydrogen atom in water, (CH3)2HN: ||||| 
H–O–H. Trimethylamine, dimethylamine, and methylamine each 
has one hydrogen acceptor (the lone pair of electrons on nitro-
gen) and zero, one, and two hydrogen donors, respectively; their 
respective TPSA values are 3, 12, and 26 Å2. Hydrogen bonding to 
water affects membrane permeation by passive diffusion because 
only the desolvated (water-free) form on the nonionized drug 
can permeate membranes with a high rate of passive diffusion. 
Accordingly, the rate of membrane permeation by passive diffu-
sion (and the extent of oral absorption of nontransported drugs) 
decreases with increasing TPSA (Palm et al., 1997; Price et al., 
2009; Smith and Dalvie, 2012). As a general rule, a TPSA > 75 
Å2 restricts membrane permeation by passive diffusion of acidic 
drugs, whereas a TPSA > 100 Å2 restricts membrane perme-
ation by passive diffusion of basic drugs. The difference reflects 
the property of phospholipid membranes, which are negatively 
charged. As such, basic drugs are attracted electrostatically to 
membranes, whereas acidic drugs are repelled. In effect, the equi-
librium between the ionized and nonionized form of a drug occurs 
close to the membrane in the case of basic drugs and away from 
the membrane in the case of acidic drugs. Transporters play a key 
role in the disposition of drugs with a high TPSA. If any drug 
with a TPSA greater than 140 Å2 is absorbed from the intestine 
1Alternative names for the transporters mentioned throughout this chapter are

(SLC47A1), MATE2-K (SLC47A2), MRP1 (ABCC1), MRP2 (ABCC2),

MRP7 (ABCC10), MRP8 (ABCC11), NTCP (SLC10A1), OAT1 (SLC22A6), 
OAT3 (SLC22A8), OATP1A2 (SLCO1A2), OATP1B1 (SLCO1B1),

(SLC22A2), P-gp (ABCB1 and MDR1), and URAT1 (SLC22A12).

and distributes into tissues, it is probably a substrate for one or 
more transporters (Palm et al., 1997; Ertl et al., 2000; Shitara 
et al., 2013).
The roles of passive diffusion, transport, and biotransforma-
tion in the disposition of acidic and basic drugs in humans (based 
on studies of drug pharmacokinetics) are compared and contrasted 
in Figs. 6-1 and 6-2, respectively. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory 
drugs (NSAIDs) are carboxylic acid-containing drugs with physi-
cochemical properties that generally favor membrane permeation 
by passive diffusion (they have pKa(A) values ranging from 2.5 to 
5.0 with log P values >1 and, in most cases, TPSA values <75 Å2). 
Likewise, antidepressant drugs are aliphatic amine–containing 
drugs with physicochemical properties that favor membrane per-
meation by passive diffusion (in most cases they have pKa(B) values 
in the 8.5 to 10.5 range with log P values >1 and, with few excep-
tions, TPSA values <100 Å2). As shown in Table 6-1, all NSAIDs 
have a small volume of distribution (0.1 to 0.3 L/kg, which is less 
than the volume of body water) and they have a short Tmax (the time 
to reach maximum plasma levels after oral dosing). The low volume 
of distribution of NSAIDs is attributable partly to their extensive 
binding to albumin and other plasma proteins (>99% in the major-
ity of cases) and partly to their extensive ionization (>99%) at pH 
7.4, both of which disfavor passive diffusion into tissues. The short 
Tmax of some NSAIDs is consistent with their absorption from the 
stomach (pH 1.5 to 3 in the fasted state) and duodenum (pH 5 to 6), 
two sites with low pH, which favors the nonionized form of NSAIDs 
and, hence, their absorption by passive diffusion. Oral absorption 
of NSAIDs is delayed in the fed state due, in part, to the increase 
in gastric pH (to pH 3 to 5) and the delay in gastric emptying time.
In contrast, as shown in Table 6-2, antidepressant drugs have 
a large volume of distribution (>10 L/kg, which is more than 
total body water) and, in most cases, a relatively long Tmax. The 
large volume of distribution of antidepressants is attributable to 
their rapid permeation of tissues by passive diffusion and their 
extensive partitioning into phospholipid membranes and ion par-
titioning of the charged (cationic) species into acidic organelles 
like lysosomes (pH 4 to 5) and, to a lesser extent, endosomes 
(pH 5 to 6). Following their absorption from the small intestine, 
antidepressants and many other classes of cationic amphiphilic 
drugs (CADs) reach high organ-to-blood levels (>10-fold) in the 
liver, lung, and kidney, the organs with the highest concentration 
of phospholipids and lysosomes. Ion partitioning of such drugs 
into lysosomes (and their direct complexing with phospholipids) 
often impairs the lysosomal metabolism of phospholipids and 
mucopolysaccharides, which is why CADs are renowned for 
causing phospholipidosis and/or mucopolysaccharidosis. CADs 
(positively charged) are also renowned for adsorbing to melanin, 
a negatively charged pigment in the uveal gland of the eye and 
other pigmented cells.
Transporters, such as OATP2B1 and OATP1A2, play an 
important role in the intestinal absorption of acidic drugs with 
a high TPSA, such as statins, many of which permeate mem-
branes with a low rate of passive diffusion (Shirasaka et al., 
2010, 2011; Varma et al., 2011). They also play a key role in 
the intestinal absorption of certain basic drugs, such as aliskerin, 
atenolol, and celiprolol, and zwitterionic drugs, such as cipro-
floxacin and fexofenadine—drugs whose intestinal absorption by 
OATPs is partially inhibited by large volumes of orange and apple

Biotransformation of Xenobiotics
Chapter 6
197
juice (Dolton et al., 2012). The systemic clearance of statins and 
other drugs with a high TPSA (including certain basic and zwit-
terionic drugs) is largely determined by transporter (OATP1B1 
and OATP1B3)-mediated uptake into liver, followed by hepatic 
biotransformation or transporter-mediated efflux into bile (by 
P-glycoprotein [P-gp], BCRP, MRP2, BSEP, and/or MATE1) 
(Shitara et al., 2013). Acidic drugs that undergo biliary excretion 
(i.e., transporter-mediated uptake from blood coupled with trans-
porter-mediated efflux into bile) can potentially be reabsorbed 
by intestinal uptake transporters, which gives rise to enterohe-
patic circulation. Such recycling of acidic drugs is analogous to 
the enterohepatic circulation of bile acids (lipophilic carboxylic 
acids), which are synthesized in the liver, effluxed into bile by 
BSEP, reabsorbed from the small intestine by ASBT (in conjunc-
tion with OSTα/β), and actively transported back into the liver by 
NTCP and OATP1B1 to begin the process again (Kuipers et al., 
2014). Transporter (ASBT)-mediated reabsorption in the kidney 
similarly prevents the loss of bile acids in urine due to glomerular 
filtration.
Because carboxylic acid–containing drugs are extensively ion-
ized at pH 7.4, they are predicted NOT to enter tissues with a high 
rate of passive diffusion, which is consistent with their low volume 
of distribution. However, various acidic drugs such as NSAIDs, 
statins, and the anticonvulsant valproic acid enter the brain due to 
transport by OATPs (including OATP2B2 and OATP1A2, which are 
also expressed in the small intestine) and/or one or more members 
of the family of monocarboxylate transporters (MCTs) (Sanchez-
Covarrubias et al., 2014; Vijay and Morris, 2014). Uptake transport-
ers (OATs) also play a role in the tubular secretion of NSAIDs and 
other acid drugs. Biotransformation might be assumed to play a 
negligible role in the disposition of acidic drugs but, in many cases, 
biotransformation plays a key role in the disposition of acidic drugs 
and the metabolites of acidic drugs (particularly acyl glucuronides) 
play a key role in the toxicity and drug interaction potential of acidic 
drugs (discussed in the sections “Glucuronidation and Formation of 
Acyl-CoA Thioesters” and “CYP2C8”). Transporters also play a 
key role in the disposition of zwitterionic drugs. Zwitterionic drugs 
are predominantly cations in the stomach, which does not favor 
their absorption by passive diffusion, and are both negatively and 
positively charged in the small intestine and blood, which disfavors 
intestinal absorption and tissue distribution by passive permeation. 
Certain zwitterionic drugs target the brain, such as the anticonvul-
sants gabapentin and pregabalin, which are transported across the 
blood–brain barrier by members of the family of large amino acid 
transporters (LATs).
Interestingly, many antihistamines are also lipophilic aliphatic 
amines (pKa(B) > 8) that, like antidepressants, have physicochemi-
cal properties that favor membrane permeation with a high rate of 
passive diffusion (i.e., log P >1 and TPSA <100 Å2). Some antihis-
tamines, such as chlorpheniramine and diphenhydramine, readily 
enter the CNS and cause sedation, but others are prevented from 
entering the CNS because they are substrates for the efflux trans-
porter P-gp (a component of the blood–brain barrier). Desloratadine 
and terfenadine are examples of lipophilic amines that, because they 
are P-gp substrates, are nonsedating antihistamines (Raeissi et al., 
1999; Chen et al., 2003). Many drugs classified as neutrals contain 
an amine group with a pKa(B) < 7; they are predominantly nonion-
ized at pH 7.4. Aromatic amines and amides fall into this class. 
Many of them permeate membranes with a high rate of passive 
diffusion and biotransformation plays a key role in their elimina-
tion. However, in contrast to amine-containing drugs with a pKa(B) 
> 8, amine-containing drugs with a pKa(B) < 7 do not partition

extensively into phospholipid membranes or lysosomes; hence, they 
have moderate volumes of distribution (usually <5 L/kg) in contrast 
to the aliphatic amines classified as CADs (with volumes of distri-
bution >10 L/kg).
These considerations apply to all xenobiotics, not just drugs. 
For example, dioxin permeates membranes with a high rate of 
passive diffusion, which is consistent with its lack of ionizable 
groups at physiologic pH values, and its high log P (>5) and 
low TPSA (18 Å2). But there are exceptions (or apparent excep-
tions) especially among certain natural products. For example, the 
aminoglycoside antibiotics gentamicin, tobramycin, and amika-
cin contain four or five cationic sites, and have strongly negative 
log P values and very high TPSA values (200 to 330 Å2). They 
are administered intravenously or intramuscularly due to their 
low oral bioavailability. Aminoglycosides do not diffuse through 
membranes but they get into various cells by a passive process, 
namely endocytosis: an invagination of the plasma membrane 
that occurs when the four or five positively charged sites on the 
aminoglycoside form a complex with a corresponding number of 
negatively charged sites on the phospholipid membrane. Some 
drugs, such as sildenafil (Viagra), permeate membranes with a 
higher rate of passive diffusion than would be predicted from their 
TPSA value, but this is due to internal hydrogen bonding, which 
effectively reduces TPSA, a measure of external hydrogen bond-
ing to water. In the case of sildenafil, internal hydrogen bonding 
reduces its predicted TPSA by 20 Å2 (from 118 to 98 Å2), which 
enables sildenafil to be completely absorbed from the intestine. 
However, oral bioavailability of parent drug is less than 100% 
due to presystemic biotransformation of sildenafil in the small 
intestine and liver.
The final point in the preceding paragraph warrants further 
consideration because several factors, including biotransformation, 
can contribute to an absolute oral bioavailability of less than 100%. 
Absolute oral bioavailability is usually determined by comparing 
systemic exposure to parent drug following oral administration 
(AUCpo) and intravenous (IV) administration (AUCiv) with adjust-
ment for dose (if necessary):
Absolute oral bioavailability
AUC
Dose
AUC
Dose
po
iv
iv
p
(%) =
⋅
⋅
⋅
100
o
where AUC is the area under the plasma concentration versus time 
curve, a measure of systemic exposure. Absolute oral bioavailabil-
ity is a pharmacokinetic term that applies only to the parent drug. 
There are several reasons why absolute oral bioavailability may be 
less than 100%:
	 1.	 The drug may be partially or completely insoluble in the 
gastrointestinal tract at the dose administered orally. In this 
case, absorption of both parent drug and drug-derived mate-
rial (parent + metabolites) is less than 100%. In this case, oral 
absorption may decrease with increasing dose (due to precipi-
tation of the drug at high doses), which gives rise to an infra-­
proportional dose–exposure relationship.
	 2.	 The drug may permeate membranes or pass between cells with 
a low rate of passive diffusion and the drug is not efficiently 
transported by an intestinal uptake transporter. Once again, 
oral absorption of both parent drug and drug-derived mate-
rial is less than 100%. The intestine is the therapeutic target 
for some drugs, such as linaclotide, an agonist of gluanylate 
cyclase 2 (expressed on the cell surface of intestinal epithe-
lial cells) and is used to treat constipation and irritable bowel 
syndrome. Linaclotide has a very low log P (close to ‒6) and 
very high TPSA (726 Å2)—properties that strongly disfavor 
passive permeation of membranes—and is not absorbed from 
the gastrointestinal tract.
	 3.	 The drug may permeate membranes with a high rate of passive 
diffusion or be efficiently transported by an intestinal uptake 
transporter, but it is biotransformed in the small intestine, liver 
or, in principle, the lung (three presystemic organs). In this 
case, oral absorption of parent drug is less than 100%, but oral 
absorption of drug-derived material (parent + metabolites) is 
100% (all of the drug is absorbed but the parent drug under-
goes presystemic biotransformation). Sildenafil is one of a 
large number of drugs that are completely absorbed from the 
intestine but undergoes significant presystemic (or first-pass) 
metabolism.
	 4.	 The drug may permeate membranes with a high rate of passive 
diffusion or is a substrate for an intestinal uptake transporter, 
but its intestinal absorption is restricted by one or more intesti-
nal efflux transporters, such as P-gp. For highly soluble, highly 
permeable drugs, efflux by intestinal transporters can be satu-
rated at high doses, which gives rise to a supra-proportional 
dose–exposure relationship.
In the case of drugs that are substrates for both the intestinal 
efflux transporter P-gp and the drug-metabolizing enzyme CYP3A 
(the major cytochrome P450 enzyme in the intestine and a major 
enzyme in the liver), oral absorption of parent drug can be espe-
cially reduced because efflux by P-gp slows the rate of intestinal 
absorption, which increases the extent of metabolism by intesti-
nal CYP3A (Raeissi et al., 1999; Benet, 2009). This transporter–
enzyme interplay underscores the importance of BOTH transporters 
and drug-metabolizing enzymes to the overall disposition of certain 
drugs and other xenobiotics.
Extrinsic factors that impact the oral bioavailability of drugs 
include (1) aqueous solubility, which is affected by pH, food, 
bile acids, and drug formulation); (2) uptake by intestinal trans-
porters, which are inhibited by compounds in apple and orange 
juice, and uptake by hepatic transporters, which are inhibited 
by drugs such as rifampin); (3) efflux by intestinal transporters 
such as P-gp, which can be inhibited by some drugs and induced 
by others; and (4) biotransformation by intestinal enzymes such 
as CYP3A, which is inhibited by compounds in grapefruit juice, 
and hepatic enzymes, many of which are inhibited by some drugs 
and induced by others. In addition to drugs that cause inhibition 
or induction, genetic polymorphism (an intrinsic factor) can also 
affect the activity of transporters and drug-metabolizing enzymes. 
For a growing number of transporters and drug-­metabolizing 
enzymes, genetic polymorphisms have been identified that 
decrease their activity (loss-of-function allelic variants or gene 
deletion) or increase it (gain-of-function allelic variants or gene 
duplication).
Biotransformation has traditionally been divided into two 
phases. Phase 1 biotransformation involves oxidation, reduction, 
and hydrolysis, which result in the introduction or exposure of func-
tional groups such as –OH, –NH2, –SH, or –COOH. Phase 2 bio-
transformation involves conjugation of the xenobiotic or its Phase 
1 metabolite(s) with an acid (glucuronic acid or sulfonic acid) or 
zwitterion (glutathione or amino acids such as glycine, taurine, and 
glutamine). Phase 1 biotransformation facilitates the elimination 
of xenobiotics by increasing their TPSA and lowering their log P, 
both of which decrease membrane permeation by passive diffu-
sion and decrease the likelihood of the excreted metabolites being

Biotransformation of Xenobiotics
Chapter 6
199
reabsorbed from the small intestine or kidney. Phase 1 biotransfor-
mation also produces metabolites that potentially undergo Phase 2 
biotransformation. Phase 2 biotransformation facilitates the elimi-
nation of xenobiotics by adding an ionizable group (often an acidic 
moiety that is predominantly [>99%] negatively charged above pH 
7) that is associated with relatively large increases in TPSA and 
decreases in log P. Phase 1 biotransformation increases TPSA by up 
to 24 Å2 and decreases log P by up to 1.5 units. Phase 2 biotransfor-
mation increases TPSA by as much as about 160 Å2 and decreases 
log P by as much as 3.5 units. The effects of common pathways 
of Phase 1 and Phase 2 biotransformation on TPSA and log P are 
summarized in Table 6-3.
In general, the physicochemical properties of metabolites 
formed by Phase 1 biotransformation are similar to those of the 
parent compound because Phase 1 biotransformation results in 
relatively modest changes in TPSA and log P (Table 6-3). In 
the case of drugs with high passive permeability, their Phase 
1 metabolites (formed in the small intestine or liver) are often 
present in the systemic circulation (Smith and Dalvie, 2012). 
In fact several drugs on the market today are pharmacologically 
active Phase 1 metabolites of previously approved drugs (such 
as desloratadine, the N-dealkylated metabolite of loratadine). In 
contrast, conjugated metabolites formed by Phase 2 biotransfor-
mation do not permeate membranes with a high rate of passive 
diffusion. Their elimination from the site of formation is medi-
ated by transporters. In the case of Phase 2 metabolites formed 
in the liver, the conjugates can be effluxed into bile by transport-
ers on the canalicular membrane (such as MRP2 and BCRP) or 
effluxed into blood (with subsequent elimination in urine) by 
transporters on the sinusoidal membrane (such as MRP1, MRP3, 
MRP4, MRP5, and MRP6) (Zamek-Gliszczynski et al., 2006). 
The efflux of Phase 2 metabolites into bile versus blood (the 
so-called vectorial route of elimination) can occur in a species-
dependent manner. In the same species, treatment with drugs 
that induce the expression of transporters can affect the vectorial 
route of elimination of Phase 2 metabolites (Slitt et al., 2003). 
Accordingly, Phase 2 metabolites of drugs can sometimes be 
observed as circulating metabolites in humans but not rodents 
(or vice versa) even though humans and rodents form the same 
Phase 2 metabolite.

200
Disposition of toxicants
unit II
Conjugations with glucuronic acid, sulfonic acid, glutathione, 
and various amino acids are not the only Phase 2 biotransformation 
reactions. Acetylation and methylation are Phase 2 biotransforma-
tion reactions but with a notable difference: they do not add an 
ionizable group to the xenobiotic. In contrast to all other pathways 
of Phase 2 biotransformation, methylation actually decreases TPSA 
and increases log P (Table 6-3), which can slow the overall rate 
of xenobiotic elimination from the body. Acetylation of a basic 
amine (R–NH2 → R–NH–CO–CH3) has a modest effect on TPSA 
and log P (Table 6-3), but it can greatly lower the pKa(B) of the 
nitrogen atom from one that favors partitioning into phospholipid 
membranes and lysosomes (pKa(B) > 8) to one that does not sup-
port such partitioning (pKa(B) << 7). In this case, acetylation can 
decrease the volume of distribution and thereby promote xenobiotic 
elimination.
By facilitating the elimination of Phase 2 metabolites in bile or 
urine (via blood), transporters play an important role in the elimi-
nation of xenobiotics that permeate membranes with a high rate of 
passive diffusion, although the rate of metabolism generally deter-
mines the overall rate of elimination of the xenobiotic (i.e., metab-
olism represents the rate-limiting step in elimination). As noted 
above, the interplay between intestinal efflux transporters like P-gp 
and intestinal drug-metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A can increase 
the extent of metabolism and decrease oral absorption of highly 
permeable drugs and other xenobiotics. What about xenobiotics that 
permeate membranes with a low rate of passive diffusion, such as 
drugs with a high TPSA, but which are absorbed, distributed, and 
eliminated because they are substrates for transporters? It might be 
assumed that drugs whose systemic clearance depends on facili-
tated transport (i.e., drugs for which transporter-mediated uptake 
into liver or kidney represents the rate-limiting step in systemic 
clearance) are slowly biotransformed if, in fact, they are biotrans-
formed at all. It seems reasonable to assume that drug-metabolizing 
enzymes would have evolved NOT to metabolize low permeability 
xenobiotics because such compounds already have a high TPSA 
and/or low log P, so there would be little advantage to increasing it 
further. It is perhaps surprising, therefore, that several drugs whose 
permeation into liver is dependent on uptake transporters (namely, 
OATP1B1 and OATP1B3) are among the most rapidly metabolized 
drugs by the human liver microsomal cytochrome P450 (Phase 1 
biotransformation), as shown in Fig. 6-3.
This feature may have evolved for the following reason. In the 
case of xenobiotics that permeate membranes with a high rate of 
passive diffusion, the unbound concentration of xenobiotic inside 
the cell cannot be greater than the unbound concentration outside 
the cells because they are in rapid equilibrium. However, in the 
case of xenobiotics that do not permeate cells rapidly by passive 
diffusion but are actively transported into cells (such as cells in the 
small intestine and liver), the unbound intracellular concentration 
can be much greater than the unbound extracellular concentration. 
(Emphasis is placed on the unbound concentration because the total 
intracellular concentration of a xenobiotic, including those that 
permeate membranes with a high rate of passive diffusion, is usu-
ally much higher than its total extracellular concentration because 
xenobiotics inside the cell partition into phospholipid membranes 
and organelles and/or bind extensively to various proteins and other 
cellular constituents.) A xenobiotic that is transported into the small 
intestine or liver can be eliminated either by transporter-mediated 
efflux or by biotransformation. Many of the drugs that are trans-
ported into the liver, such as the statins, are indeed effluxed into 
bile by canalicular transporters. But high rates of biotransforma-
tion of xenobiotics that are substrates for uptake transporters may 
have evolved as a second mechanism to protect cells from high and 
potentially toxic intracellular levels of xenobiotics that are trans-
ported into the liver. When xenobiotics with low passive permeabil-
ity are transported into hepatocytes, it seems reasonable to assume 
that their unbound intracellular concentration would be higher than 
its unbound extracellular concentration. But several drugs with 
these properties are metabolized so quickly (in addition to being 
effluxed into bile) that their intracellular unbound concentration is 
actually lower than their unbound extracellular concentration.
CYP3A enzymes are often involved in the biotransforma-
tion of drugs that are substrates for the efflux transporter P-gp or 
are substrates for the hepatic OATP uptake transporters. As noted 
above, the interplay between intestinal P-gp and intestinal CYP3A 
increases the extent of biotransformation of various drugs during 
their intestinal absorption and, as shown in Fig. 6-3, some of the 
drugs transported into the liver by OAPT1B1/3 are rapidly metabo-
lized by hepatic CYP3A enzymes. CYP3A is unusual for its abil-
ity to metabolize high-molecular-weight xenobiotics with a high 
TPSA, but it is not alone. Many acidic drugs with a high molecular 
weight and high TPSA (including glucuronide conjugates of drugs) 
are substrates for CYP2C8. CYP2C9 also metabolizes numer-
ous acidic drugs but with a preference for low-molecular-weight 
compounds. Humans and cynomolgus monkeys evolved CYP2C 
enzymes capable of biotransforming acidic drugs. In contrast, dogs 
are renowned for expressing CYP enzymes that, in general, do not 
biotransform acidic drugs. Rats and mice (the other two non-clinical 
species widely used in drug safety and toxicological testing) are 
intermediate in their ability to biotransform acidic drugs by CYP 
enzymes. It is difficult to know what evolutionary pressures led to 
these species differences in CYP-dependent biotransformation of 
acidic compounds, but gastric pH offers a potential insight. Humans 
and cynomolgus monkeys have low gastric pH (pH 1.5 and 2.1, 
respectively) compared with mice, rats, and dogs (pH 3.8, 4.4, 
and 4.5, respectively); hence, humans and cynomolgus monkeys 
are more likely to absorb acidic xenobiotics by passive diffusion

human liver microsomes) are from Evans et al. (2003) for eletriptan, from 
Hyland et al. (2001) for sildenafil, from De Bruyn et al. (2016) for lopinavir,

are drugs whose plasma AUC increase fivefold or more when coadminis-

at the University of Washington Drug Interaction Database (https://www.
druginteractioninfo.org).

Biotransformation of Xenobiotics
Chapter 6
201
from the stomach than many other species (Beasley et al., 2015). 
It is conceivable that species differences in gastric pH provided an 
evolutionary pressure for the selection of acid-metabolizing CYP 
enzymes in humans and cynomolgus monkeys. Species differences 
in gastric pH are partly responsible for species differences in the 
composition of the intestinal microbiome (Beasley et al., 2015), 
which in turn influences the biotransformation of certain xenobiot-
ics and bile acids (Klaassen and Cui, 2015).
In summary, the overall fate or disposition of a xenobiotic, 
which encompasses its absorption, distribution, metabolism, 
and elimination (ADME), is determined by three major factors, 
namely passive diffusion, facilitated transport, and biotrans-
formation (metabolism). In most cases, all three processes are 
involved. Xenobiotics that rapidly permeate membranes by pas-
sive diffusion are biotransformed inside cells. Such xenobiotics 
do not require uptake transporters to enter cells, but they require 
efflux transporters to facilitate the elimination of certain metabo-
lites, notably those formed by Phase 2 biotransformation reac-
tions (except methylation and acetylation). Xenobiotics with low 
passive permeability that are transported into cells, resulting in 
potentially high intracellular concentrations, can be eliminated 
by efflux transporters, but in several cases they are rapidly bio-
transformed to lower their intracellular concentration and further 
facilitate their elimination by efflux transporters. A few xenobiot-
ics permeate membranes with a high rate of passive diffusion but 
they are neither substrates for Phase 1 or Phase 2 biotransforming 
enzymes nor substrates for uptake and efflux transporters. Such 
xenobiotics will be absorbed quickly but eliminated slowly, as is 
the case with dioxin and other persistent polyhalogenated aro-
matic hydrocarbons.
This chapter describes some fundamental principles of xeno-
biotic biotransformation, and describes the major enzyme systems 
involved in the biotransformation (or metabolism) of drugs and 
other xenobiotics. The examples given are biased toward drugs and 
human enzyme systems for two reasons. First, many of the funda-
mental principles of xenobiotic biotransformation stem from such 
studies. This is especially true of drugs with a narrow therapeutic 
index (where the toxic dose is not much greater than the thera-
peutic dose), which have revealed a large number of genetic and 
environmental factors that affect xenobiotic biotransformation and, 
hence, drug toxicity. Second, adverse drug events (ADEs) are one 
of the leading causes of death in the United States. Lazarou et al. 
(1998) estimated that in 1994 over 2 million hospitalized patients 
had serious ADEs (also known as adverse drug reactions or ADRs) 
and 106,000 had fatal outcomes, placing ADEs only behind heart 
disease, cancer, stroke, and pulmonary disease as a leading cause of 
death. Of the 548 new chemical entities approved by the Food and 
Drug Administration (FDA) between 1974 and 1999, about 10% 
(56 drugs) required black box warnings or were withdrawn from 
the market because of ADEs that included hepatotoxicity, cardio-
toxicity (several as a result of drug–drug interactions), myelotox-
icity, immunotoxicity, and warnings to pregnant women (Lasser 
et al., 2002; Thomas, 2002). Between 1998 and 2005 the number of 
serious ADEs reported voluntarily to the FDA through the Adverse 
Events Reporting System (AERS), known as MedWatch reports, 
increased 2.6-fold (from 34,966 in 1998 to 89,842 in 2005) and 
the number of ADE-related fatalities increased 2.7-fold (from 5519 
in 1998 to 15,107 in 2005) (Moore et al., 2007). These increases 
in ADE incidence were four times greater than the increase in the 
total number of outpatient prescriptions written during the same 
period. In many cases, xenobiotic biotransformation is central to 
understanding these ADEs.
PRINCIPLES OF XENOBIOTIC 
BIOTRANSFORMATION
It is difficult to make categorical statements about xenobiotic 
biotransformation because there is an exception to every rule. 
Nevertheless, the following points, which might be considered 
principles or rules, apply in the majority of cases:
Point 1 Xenobiotic biotransformation or drug metabolism is the 
process of converting lipophilic (fat-soluble) chemicals, which are 
readily absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract and other sites, into 
hydrophilic (water-soluble) chemicals, which are readily excreted 
in urine or bile. There are exceptions even to this most basic rule. 
For example, acetylation and methylation are biotransformation 
reactions that result in a nominal increase or a small decrease in the 
water solubility of xenobiotics, as shown in Table 6-3. As a general 
rule, xenobiotics with a log D7.4 > 0 require biotransformation to 
facilitate their elimination (Williams et al., 2003a).
Point 2 The biotransformation of xenobiotics is catalyzed by 
various enzyme systems that can be divided into four categories 
based on the reaction they catalyze:
	 1.	 Hydrolysis (e.g., carboxylesterase)
	 2.	 Reduction (e.g., carbonyl reductase)
	 3.	 Oxidation (e.g., cytochrome P450 [CYP])
	 4.	 Conjugation (e.g., UDP-glucuronosyltransferase [UGT])
The mammalian enzymes involved in the hydrolysis, reduction, 
oxidation, and conjugation of xenobiotics are listed in Table 6-4, 
together with their principal subcellular location. The conjugation 
reactions include glucuronidation, sulfonation (often called sulfa-
tion), acetylation, methylation, conjugation with glutathione (GSH; 
mercapturic acid synthesis), and conjugation with amino acids (such 
as glycine, taurine, and glutamine). Examples of the major chemical 
groups that undergo biotransformation together with the enzymes 
that commonly mediate their biotransformation are given in Table 6-5 
(Williams et al., 2003a; Testa and Krämer, 2008, 2010).
Xenobiotic biotransformation is generally catalyzed by 
enzymes, but there are exceptions. For example, hydrolysis of cer-
tain carboxylic and phosphoric acid esters, reduction of sulfoxides 
to sulfides (e.g., rabeprazole), isomerization involving enol–keto 
tautomerization (e.g., thalidomide), and the conjugation of certain 
xenobiotics with GSH can occur nonenzymatically at an appre-
ciable rate. Certain reactions are catalyzed by gastric acid, such 
as the hydrolysis of esters and the conversion of indole-3-carbinol 
to a dimer that is a potent agonist of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor 
(AhR) and, consequently, an inducer of various enzymes including 
certain CYP enzymes (CYP1A1, CYP1A2, CYP1B1, and CYP2S1) 
(Grose and Bjeldanes, 1992).
Point 3 In general, individual xenobiotic-biotransforming 
enzymes are located in a single organelle. In Table 6-4, some 
enzymes are listed with two or more subcellular locations. 
However, in such cases, the enzyme name generally refers to two or 
more enzymes, each with its own distinct subcellular location. For 
example, the epoxide hydrolase located in microsomes is the same 
enzyme located in the plasma membrane but is a different enzyme 
from the epoxide hydrolase located in cytosol (i.e., they are distinct 
gene products). From a practical perspective, it is noteworthy that 
during the homogenization of tissue and the preparation of subcel-
lular fractions, a certain degree of cross-contamination of organelles 
occurs. For example, microsomes contain detectable levels of mono-
amine oxidase (MAO) and other outer mitochondrial enzymes, such 
as the mitochondrial amidoxime-reducing component (mARC) due

202
Disposition of toxicants
unit II
to their contamination with the outer mitochondrial membrane. In 
contrast, some xenobiotic-biotransforming enzymes are present in 
two or more subcellular locations. Some CYP enzymes, such as 
CYP2E1, are present in microsomes and the inner mitochondrial 
membrane, which has implications for the site of xenobiotic toxic-
ity, as discussed in the section “CYP2E1.”
Point 4 In general, xenobiotic biotransformation is accom-
plished by a limited number of enzymes with broad substrate 
specificities. In humans, for example, two CYP enzymes—namely, 
CYP2D6 and CYP3A4—metabolize 40% of drugs (n = 860) and 
21% of natural products/endobiotics (n = 331 compounds) (Rendic 
and Guengerich, 2015). The broad and sometimes overlapping sub-
strate specificities of xenobiotic-biotransforming enzymes preclude 
the possibility of naming the individual enzymes after the reac-
tions they catalyze (which is how most other enzymes are named). 
Many of the enzymes involved in xenobiotic biotransformation 
are arranged in families and subfamilies and named according to 
nomenclature systems based on the primary amino acid sequence of 
the individual enzymes. CYP2D6, for example, is in CYP family 2, 
subfamily D, and gene number 6. Some enzymes are given the same 
name across all mammalian species, whereas others are named in a 
species-specific manner. For example, some CYP enzymes, such as 
CYP1A1, CYP1A2, and CYP1B1, are so named in all mammalian 
species, whereas CYP enzymes in the CYP2, CYP3, and CYP4 
families (with certain exceptions such as CYP2E1) are named in a 
species-specific manner. The convention of using italic and regular

Disposition of toxicants
unit II
letters to distinguish between the gene and gene products (mRNA 
and protein), respectively, and the convention of using lower case 
letters to designate mouse genes and gene products will not be fol-
lowed in this chapter.
The structure (i.e., amino acid sequence) of a given xenobiotic-
biotransforming enzyme may differ among individuals, which can 
give rise to differences in rates of drug metabolism. In general, a 
variant form of a xenobiotic-biotransforming enzyme (known as an 
allelic variant or allelozyme) has diminished enzymatic activity or 
level of expression compared with that of the wild-type enzyme, 
although this is not always the case (see the sections “Alcohol 
Dehydrogenase,” “Aldehyde Oxidase,” “Cholinesterases (AChE 
and BChE),” “Carboxylesterases,” “Epoxide Hydrolases,” “Flavin 
Monooxygenases,” “Glutathione Conjugation,” “Paraoxonases 
(Lactonases),” “Sulfonation,” “CYP1A1, CYP1A2, and CYP1B1,” 
“CYP2C8,” “CYP2C19,” and “CYP3A5”). The impact of amino 
acid substitution(s) on the catalytic activity of a xenobiotic-bio-
transforming enzyme may be substrate-dependent, such that an 
allelic variant may interact normally with some substrates (and 
inhibitors) but interact atypically with others.
The broad substrate specificity of xenobiotic-biotransforming 
enzymes makes them catalytically versatile but slow compared with 
most other enzymes (with the exception of hydrolytic reactions). 
The sequential oxidation, conjugation, and transport of a xenobiotic 
tend to proceed quicker at each subsequent step, which prevents the 
accumulation of intracellular metabolites.
Point 5 Hydrolysis, reduction, and oxidation expose or intro-
duce a functional group (such as −OH, −NH2, −SH, or −COOH) 
that can be converted to a water-soluble conjugate (see Table 6-5). 
For subsequent conjugation, the functional group introduced or 
exposed by hydrolysis, reduction, or oxidation must be nucleophilic 
(in the case of glucuronidation, sulfonation, methylation, acetyla-
tion, and conjugation with glycine or taurine) or electrophilic (in 
the case of glutathionylation). The first three reactions (hydrolysis, 
reduction, and oxidation) are called Phase 1 reactions, and the con-
jugation reactions are called Phase 2 reactions. These terms were 
originally coined to distinguish biotransformation reactions that 
could potentially increase the toxicity of a xenobiotic (by forming 
a reactive metabolite), which was attributed to Phase 1 biotrans-
formation, from those that resulted in only detoxication, which 
was attributed to Phase 2 biotransformation (Williams, 1959). 
However, on a case-by-case basis, ALL biotransformation reactions 
are capable of increasing the toxicity of xenobiotics. Formation of 
hepatotoxic acyl glucuronides of carboxylic acid–containing drugs 
such as NSAIDs involves conjugation with glucuronic acid (a Phase 
2 biotransformation reaction). In the United States, NSAIDs and 
other carboxylic acid–containing drugs represent the largest cat-
egory of drugs whose clinical development was halted or whose 
regulatory approval was subsequently withdrawn because of liver 
toxicity in humans (Fung et al., 2001). Although Phase 1 biotrans-
formation often precedes Phase 2 metabolism, some drugs, such 
as acetaminophen (Tylenol), are conjugated directly (by glucuron-
idation and sulfonation), whereas other drugs, such as the lipid-
lowering drug gemfibrozil and the NSAID diclofenac, undergo 
Phase 1 metabolism (by CYP [CYP2C8]) after Phase 2 metabolism 
(glucuronidation) (Ogilvie et al., 2006; Tornio et al., 2014).
Point 6 Oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis, methylation, and 
acetylation generally cause a modest increase (or a small decrease 
in the case of methylation) in the water solubility of a xenobiotic, 
whereas glucuronidation, sulfonation, glutathionylation, and amino 
acid conjugation generally cause a marked increase in hydrophilic-
ity, as shown in Table 6-3. The impact of biotransformation on the 
physiochemical properties and disposition of xenobiotics and their 
metabolites has been discussed in the section “Introduction.” For a 
drug to be absorbed by passive diffusion in the gut (so that it can be 
orally effective and distribute throughout the body) it must generally 
have a log P of 0 to 5 and a calculated total polar surface area (TPSA) 
less than 100 Å2 (Smith and Dalvie, 2012). Water-soluble drugs 
(those with a negative log P) might be orally active if their absorption 
is mediated by an intestinal drug transporter. Extremely lipophilic 
drugs (log P > 5) tend to be too insoluble or they dissolve too slowly 
to be well absorbed from the intestine unless the dose is particularly 
low. Drugs must undergo desolvation (i.e., they must dissociate from 
water molecules) to penetrate the lipid bilayer of cells. Drugs begin 
to lose their lipoidal permeability when their TPSA increases above 
about 75 Å2 and lose it entirely at a TPSA of about 140 Å2. When 
drugs meet the aforementioned criteria (log P = 0−5, TPSA < 100 
Å2), their metabolites formed by oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis, 
methylation, or acetylation also tend to meet these same criteria; 
hence, the metabolites formed by these reactions, such as the parent 
drug, readily diffuse across biological membranes down a concentra-
tion gradient (unless their diffusion into tissues such as the brain is 
restricted by efflux transporters). Metabolites formed by conjugation 
reactions (other than methylation and acetylation) contain a nega-
tively charged glucuronide, sulfate, or amino acid (glycine or taurine) 
moiety (or a zwitterionic GSH moiety) and consequently have a much 
lower log P and a much greater TPSA than the parent drug, so much 
so that they cannot readily diffuse passively across biological mem-
branes. The elimination of such conjugates from the liver (where they 
are largely formed) requires active transport across the canalicular 
(apical) membrane so that they can be eliminated in bile or across 
the sinusoidal (basolateral) membrane, which enables their trans-
port into blood (where they are generally highly bound to albu-
min given their anionic nature [negative charge]) and then to the 
kidney for elimination in urine (by either glomerular filtration or 
possibly active transport [tubular secretion]). Drug conjugates 
are actively transported into bile primarily by MRP2 on the cana-
licular membrane and actively transported into blood primarily by 
MRP1 (in the case of GSH conjugates) and MRP3 (in the case of 
glucuronide and sulfate conjugates) on the sinusoidal membrane, 
although on a case-by-case basis other transporters may be involved 
(Borst et al., 2006).
Point 7 Not all biotransformation reactions are catalyzed by 
the mammalian enzymes listed in Table 6-4. Some biotransforma-
tion reactions are catalyzed by enzymes that participate in inter-
mediary (endobiotic) metabolism, whereas others are catalyzed by 
enzymes in the gut microbiota (largely anaerobic bacteria in the 
colon) (Klaassen and Cui, 2015). This principle is illustrated in 
Fig. 6-4 for three xenobiotics that are all converted to benzoic acid 
and then hippuric acid. The conversion of toluene to hippuric acid 
is catalyzed by some of the xenobiotic-biotransforming enzymes 
listed in Table 6-4: microsomal CYP converts toluene to benzoic 
acid in three oxidative steps (R–CH3 → R–CH2OH → RCHO → 
RCOOH), which introduces a functional group (namely, –COOH) 
that is conjugated with glycine to produce hippuric acid, which 
is excreted in urine. Cinnamic acid is also converted to benzoic 
acid but, in this case, the reaction is catalyzed by the mitochondrial 
enzymes involved in the β-oxidation of fatty acids. Quinic acid is 
also converted to benzoic acid, but this reductive, multistep reac-
tion is catalyzed by gut microbiota. Incidentally, the conversion of 
benzoic acid to hippuric acid is of historical interest because it is 
generally recognized as the first xenobiotic biotransformation reac-
tion to be discovered (in dogs by Woehler in 1828, and in humans 
by Ure in 1841).

Biotransformation of Xenobiotics
Chapter 6
205
Some drugs are intentionally designed to be biotransformed 
by endobiotic-metabolizing enzymes. For example, the anti-HIV 
drug zidovudine (AZT) is converted to a triphosphate nucleoside 
by enzymes in the salvage pathway (nucleoside kinase, nucleoside 
monophosphate kinase [NMK], and nucleoside diphosphate kinase 
[NDK]). HIV reverse transcriptase inhibitors, such as zidovudine, are 
administered as nonphosphorylated analogs to facilitate their absorp-
tion and cellular uptake. Conversely, some drugs are administered 
as a phosphorylated prodrug (e.g., fosamprenavir) to promote their 
water solubility and rate of dissolution. The luminal surface of the 
small intestine contains high levels of alkaline phosphatase, which 
hydrolyzes prodrugs such as fosamprenavir and thereby releases 
the active drug at the surface of the enterocyte, where it is readily 
absorbed. Generally speaking, kinase and alkaline phosphatase are 
not usually considered to be xenobiotic-biotransforming enzymes.
Point 8 Just as some xenobiotics are biotransformed by the 
so-called endobiotic-metabolizing enzymes (Point 7), certain endo-
biotics are biotransformed by the so-called xenobiotic-metabolizing 
enzymes. For example, the same CYP enzymes implicated in xeno-
biotic biotransformation also contribute to the hepatic catabolism 
of steroid hormones, and the same UGTs that conjugate xenobi-
otics also glucuronidate bilirubin, thyroid hormones, and steroid 
hormones. Interestingly, the CYP and UGT enzymes that play a 
major role in steroid metabolism (namely, CYP3A4 and UGT1A1) 
display positive homotropic cooperativity (i.e., the enzymes have 
Hill coefficients >1) such that the rate of metabolism of steroid 
hormones by these enzymes is slow at low substrate concentrations 
but increases dramatically as the concentration of steroid hormone 
increases. Benzoic acid, a xenobiotic, is conjugated with glycine (as 
shown in Fig. 6-4), and so are bile acids (endobiotics). Certain leu-
kotrienes are GSH conjugates. Conjugates of endogenous substrates, 
such as the glucuronide and sulfate conjugates of steroid and thyroid 
hormones, tend to be transported out of the hepatocyte by MRP2, 
the same canalicular transporter that transports drug conjugates into 
bile. From the few examples in Points 7 and 8, it is apparent that, on 
a case-by-case basis, there is often no clear-cut distinction between 
endobiotic- and xenobiotic-biotransforming enzymes.
The human genome project has helped to establish that what 
were once thought to be two distinct enzymes, one involved in the 
metabolism of endobiotics and one involved in the metabolism of 
xenobiotics, are in fact one and the same enzyme. For example, the 
microsomal enzyme known as 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase 
is identical to the xenobiotic-metabolizing enzyme known as micro-
somal carbonyl reductase (gene symbol HSD11B1; aka SDR26C1). 
Likewise, hepoxilin A3 hydrolase was thought to represent a fifth class 
of epoxide hydrolases, but was subsequently identified as the same 
enzyme as soluble epoxide hydrolase (sEH) (Cronin et al., 2011).
Point 9 Several xenobiotic-biotransforming enzymes are induc-
ible, meaning their expression can be increased (upregulated) usu-
ally in response to exposure to high concentrations of xenobiotics. 
Induction is mediated by ligand-activated receptors (so-called xeno-
sensors) that are activated by xenobiotics (ligands) to DNA-binding 
proteins that upregulate the transcription of various genes encod-
ing xenobiotic-biotransforming enzymes, especially CYP enzymes, 
which are usually induced to the greatest extent (in terms of fold 
increase). The major xenosensors are the aryl hydrocarbon receptor

206
Disposition of toxicants
unit II
(AhR), which induces CYP1 enzymes, the constitutive androstane 
receptor (CAR), which induces CYP2B, CYP2C, and CYP3A 
enzymes, the pregnane X receptor (PXR), which also induces 
CYP2B, CYP2C, and CYP3A enzymes, and the peroxisome prolif-
erator–activated receptor α (PPARα), which induces CYP4 enzymes 
in rodents. CAR and PXR are closely related nuclear receptors, and 
they tend to be activated by the same ligands and bind to the same 
DNA-response elements (i.e., the same discrete regions of DNA that 
control gene expression), for which reason there is said to be consid-
erable crosstalk between these two xenosensors. Certain xenosen-
sors are activated by endogenous ligands (e.g., bilirubin, bile acids, 
and fatty acids activate CAR, PXR, and PPARα, respectively), and 
certain nuclear receptors, such as the vitamin D receptor (VDR), 
can mimic PXR and induce CYP3A4, which inactivates the active 
metabolite of vitamin D. These examples illustrate how xenosensors 
are not just involved in xenobiotic disposition but also play a role in 
endobiotic homeostasis. Two proteins called Kelch-like ECH-related 
protein 1 (KEAP-1) and nuclear factor E2 p45-related factor-2 
(Nrf2) function as xenosensors inasmuch as they induce enzymes 
in response to oxidative stress, which is often associated with xeno-
biotic biotransformation. Binding of the transcription factor Nrf2 
to the region of DNA known as the antioxidant response element 
(also known as the electrophilic response element [EpRE]) induces 
enzymes that detoxify electrophiles and metabolites that generate 
reactive oxygen species, including glutathione transferase (GST; 
GSTA1), microsomal epoxide hydrolase (mEH), aldo-keto reductase 
(AKRs; AKR7A2 and AKR7A3, also known as aflatoxin aldehyde 
B1 reductases 1 and 2), NAD(P)H-quinone oxidoreductase (NQO1, 
also known as DT-diaphorase), microsomal heme oxygenase, and 
glutamate–­cysteine ligase (GCL), which catalyzes the rate-limiting 
step in GSH synthesis. Induction of GCL increases the rate of GSH 
synthesis under conditions of oxidative stress and a decrease in GSH 
concentration (Lee and Johnson, 2004). Experiments in wild type and 
Nrf2 knockout mice revealed that activation of AhR leads to activa-
tion of Nrf2, which contains an AhR-response element in its promoter 
region; hence, AhR agonists such as dioxin induce enzymes under the 
direct control of AhR (such as CYP1A1) as well as enzymes under 
the control of Nrf2 (such as NQO1 and the other enzymes mentioned 
above) (Yeager et al., 2009). Induction of Nrf2 via activation of AhR 
protects mice from the hepatotoxic effects of dioxin (Lu et al., 2011).
Induction is a reversible, adaptive response to xenobiotic 
exposure. The induced enzymes (and transporters) usually acceler-
ate the elimination of the xenobiotic that triggered the induction 
process, in which case the xenobiotic is said to be an autoinducer 
(one that induces its own metabolism). However, xenobiotics often 
induce enzymes that are not capable of metabolizing them, in which 
case the induction is said to be gratuitous. Induction is a pleiotro-
pic response: activation of AhR, CAR, PXR, PPARα, and Nrf2 all 
results in alterations in the expression of numerous genes.
Suppression (downregulation) of drug-metabolizing enzymes 
is often associated with inflammatory diseases (such as arthritis), 
cancer, infectious diseases (both bacterial and viral), vaccination, 
and treatment with certain proinflammatory biologics (therapeutic 
proteins). These disease processes activate nuclear factor kappa-B 
(NF-κB) (and other nuclear receptors), which suppresses the 
expression and induction of CYP and other xenobiotic-metabolizing 
enzymes because activated NF-κB suppresses all four xenosensors 
(AhR, CAR, PXR, and PPARα), as well as several other nuclear 
receptors. By reversing the disease process—such as lessening the 
inflammation associated with rheumatoid arthritis—some biolog-
ics (large drug molecules such as monoclonal antibodies and other 
types of therapeutic proteins) can reverse the suppression of drug-
metabolizing enzymes and restore their activity to normal (predis-
ease) levels (Morgan, 2009; Huang et al., 2010).
Point 10 The ability of certain xenobiotic-biotransforming 
enzymes to metabolize hormones and other endobiotics (Point 8) and 
the ability of certain xenobiotics to induce xenobiotic-biotransform-
ing enzymes (Point 9) have implications for understanding an impor-
tant mechanism by which certain xenobiotics can alter homeostasis 
or cause toxicity. Persistent exposure to xenobiotics that are enzyme 
inducers (especially CAR and PPARα activators) can increase the 
rate of steroid hormone oxidation by CYP and increase the rate of 
thyroid hormone glucuronidation and sulfonation that, in rodents, can 
lead to the development of Leydig cell tumors (due to elevated levels 
of LH and FSH) and thyroid follicular tumors (due to elevated levels 
of thyroid-stimulating hormone [TSH]), respectively (Grasso et al., 
1991). Persistent exposure to enzyme inducers can also cause liver 
tumors, although the mechanism is not fully understood. Activation 
of certain xenosensors (CAR or PPARα) is critical to liver tumor 
development in mice, although upregulation of xenobiotic-biotrans-
forming enzymes is less important than other xenosensor-dependent 
events, such as, in the case of the CAR activator phenobarbital, altered 
DNA methylation of numerous genes, upregulation of GADD45B 
(an antiapoptotic factor) and MDM2 (a negative regulator of the 
tumor suppressor p53), and the downregulation of gap junctional 
proteins (connexin 32), which diminishes cell–cell communication 
(Omiecinski et al., 2011b; Elcombe et al., 2014; Lake et al., 2015).
Phenobarbital, Wy-14643, methapyrilene, and Ponceau S are 
representatives of four classes of nongenotoxic rodent tumori-
gens (tumor promoters) that cause hepatocellular hyperplasia 
and hypertrophy in association with proliferation of the endo-
plasmic reticulum, peroxisomes, mitochondria, and lysosomes, 
respectively (Grasso et al., 1991). In the first two cases, the hepa-
tocellular changes depend on activation of CAR (in the case of 
phenobarbital) and PPARα (in the case of Wy-14643). Prolonged 
activation of these receptors in rodents results in the development 
of liver and/or thyroid tumors. However, this is thought to be a 
rodent-specific phenomenon because, in the case of phenobarbital 
and other enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (EIAEDs), there is 
compelling epidemiological evidence that these chemicals do not 
cause liver tumors in humans even after >30 years of treatment 
(Parkinson et al., 2006; Elcombe et al., 2014; Lake et al., 2015).
The major UGT responsible for conjugating bilirubin is 
UGT1A1, which is inducible by CAR activators such as pheno-
barbital and scoparone (6,7-dimethylesculetin). At one time, the 
­management of neonatal jaundice included treatment with phe-
nobarbital to induce bilirubin conjugation, but this practice has 
been discontinued. However, the Chinese herbal Yin Zhi Huang 
(active ingredient scoparone) is still used to treat neonatal jaun-
dice. Bilirubin is also a CAR agonist, and high levels of bilirubin 
induce UGT1A1 and bilirubin/bilirubin glucuronide transporters 
(MRP2 and MRP3) to increase the rate of bilirubin elimination 
when ­bilirubin levels are elevated (e.g., during hemolytic anemia).
Although prolonged activation of PXR in rats is associated 
with thyroid tumor promotion due to induction of triiodothyronine 
(T3) glucuronidation (Richardson and Klaassen, 2010), this effect 
is thought not to occur in humans. However, prolonged activation of 
PXR by rifampin in humans (which induces hepatic and intestinal 
CYP3A4) is associated with osteomalacia caused by an enhanced 
rate of conversion of the active metabolite of vitamin D to inactive 
metabolites by CYP3A4 (Xu et al., 2006; Zhou et al., 2006a).
Point 11 Xenobiotic biotransformation can alter the bio-
logical properties of a xenobiotic. It can make the xenobiotic less 
toxic (detoxication), but in some cases it can make it more toxic 
­(activation). The oxidation of ethanol (alcohol) to acetaldehyde is an 
example of xenobiotic activation, and the subsequent oxidation of 
acetaldehyde to acetic acid is an example of detoxication. The bio-
transformation of drugs can result in (1) a loss of pharmacological

Biotransformation of Xenobiotics
Chapter 6
207
activity (e.g., the conversion of acetaminophen to acetaminophen 
glucuronide and the conversion of morphine to morphine-3-glucuro-
nide), (2) no change in pharmacological activity (e.g., the conver-
sion of fluoxetine to its N-demethylated metabolite norfluoxetine), 
or (3) an increase in pharmacological activity (e.g., the conversion 
of codeine to morphine, as well as the conversion of morphine to 
morphine-6-glucuronide). When the parent drug is pharmacologi-
cally active, diminished metabolism (due to a genetic deficiency or 
enzyme inhibition) can result in an exaggerated pharmacological 
response (Type A toxicity) (Aronson and Ferner, 2003),2 such as 
the prolonged or increased hypotension caused by debrisoquine, 
alprenolol, bufuralol, metoprolol, propranolol, and timolol in indi-
viduals lacking CYP2D6 (Turner et al., 2001; Ingelman-Sundberg, 
2005). When a metabolite is the pharmacologically active compo-
nent, diminished formation (due to a genetic deficiency or enzyme 
inhibition) can result in little or no pharmacological response (Type 
F toxicity) (Aronson and Ferner, 2003), such as the diminished 
effects of codeine, an analgesic, and tamoxifen, an antiestrogen 
drug, in individuals lacking CYP2D6 and the diminished effects of 
clopidogrel, an antiplatelet drug, in individuals lacking CYP2C19 
(discussed in the sections “CYP2D6” and “CYP2C19”).
Point 12 In many cases, the toxicity of a xenobiotic is due to the 
parent compound (the compound that was absorbed), in which case 
xenobiotic biotransformation serves as a detoxication mechanism. 
This principle is underscored by the clinical observation that the 
incidence of ADEs is often higher in individuals with a poor metabo-
lizer (PM) phenotype (discussed in Point 24). However, xenobiotic-
biotransforming enzymes can convert certain xenobiotics to reactive 
metabolites (e.g., electrophiles and radicals), and this activation 
process plays an important role in chemical toxicity and chemical 
mutagenicity/carcinogenicity; the former often involves the cova-
lent binding of reactive metabolites to critical cellular nucleophiles 
such as proteins, whereas the latter involves covalent binding to one 
or more purine or pyrimidine bases in DNA. Formation of reactive 
metabolites can also lead to irreversible CYP inhibition (a process 
known as metabolism-dependent inhibition [MDI] or time-dependent 
inhibition [TDI]), which is an important cause of drug–drug inter-
actions. As illustrated in Fig. 6-5, carboxylic acid–containing drugs 
(especially the acetic and propionic acid–containing NSAIDs that 
cause liver toxicity) can be converted to reactive acyl glucuronides 
and/or acyl-CoA thioesters, whereas many neutral and basic drugs 
are converted by CYP to electrophilic metabolites or carbon-centered 
radicals that may cause liver toxicity (or damage to the cells har-
boring the activating CYP). Several nitrogen- and sulfur-containing 
drugs can be activated by peroxidases to heteroatom-centered radicals 
that can cause blood dyscrasias such as agranulocytosis/neutropenia, 
aplastic anemia, and thrombocytopenia (Tang and Lu, 2010; Walsh 
and Miwa, 2011). In Fig. 6-5, the scheme depicting the formation and 
toxicity of reactive metabolites is an oversimplification. Furthermore, 
some drugs can be converted to two or more reactive metabolites. 
The diuretic tienilic acid, for example, contains a carboxylic acid 
group that is converted to a reactive acyl glucuronide and acyl-CoA 
thioester, and a furan (a neutral group) that is converted by CYP to an 
epoxide and other reactive metabolites (such as an enedial). Tienilic 
was withdrawn from the market due to hepatotoxicity.
CYP is particularly effective at converting proximate carcino-
gens to ultimate carcinogens by converting the former to electro-
philic metabolites that bind to and mutate DNA, thereby leading to 
mutations and tumor initiation. The Ames bacterial mutagenicity 
assay, which is representative of several mutagenicity tests, is per-
formed in the absence and presence of CYP (i.e., liver microsomes) 
to evaluate the mutagenic potential of both the parent compound 
and any electrophilic metabolites, respectively. Although such in 
vitro assays help to identify compounds that can be metabolized to 
reactive metabolites, they can potentially provide misleading infor-
mation on the identity of the enzymes responsible for activating
