2
e
The sulfite produced by this reaction can be converted to 
sulfate by the molybdoenzyme, sulfite oxidase (see the section 
“Molybdenum Hydroxylases (Molybdoenzymes)”). Taking advan-
tage of the TST reaction, thiosulfate has been used as an antidote 
against cyanide poisoning (Libiad et al., 2015). Cyanide can also be 
“detoxified” by binding to methemoglobin (the oxidized or ferric 
form of hemoglobin). 4-Dimethylaminophenol is used to induce 
methemoglobinemia as an antidote to cyanide poisoning because 
methemoglobin competes with cytochrome oxidase for the cyanide 
ion. However, 4-dimethylaminophenol is nephrotoxic to rats, pre-
sumably by a mechanism similar to that described above for the 
structural analog 4-aminophenol (see the preceding section and the 
section “Glutathione Conjugation”). TST also catalyzes the trans-
fer of sulfane sulfur from glutathione persulfide (GSSH) to sulfite 
to form thiosulfate as part of the mitochondrial sulfide oxidation 
pathway (Libiad et al., 2015). The disulfide glutathione persulfide 
is formed by the transfer of sulfur from hydrogen sulfide to gluta-
thione by sulfide quinone oxidoreductase (SQR).
In humans, TST is expressed at high levels in the liver, kid-
ney, and particularly the colon where it plays a major role in the 
detoxication of hydrogen sulfide (which can reach concentrations 
of more than 3 mM) produced by anaerobic bacteria (Billaut-Laden 
et al., 2006a; Libiad et al., 2015). TST is also widely distributed in 
the central nervous system and may also play a role in the import 
of 5S rRNA into mitochondria (Smirnov et al., 2010). Several fac-
tors have been shown to induce TST, including resveratrol, α-lipoic 
acid, fungal (Phellinus linteus) polysaccharide extracts, and long-
term low-dose irradiation, implicating TST in protection against 
persistent oxidative stress (Nakajima, 2015). Six allelic variants of 
TST have been identified, namely, TST*1B to TST*1F and TST*2, 
with the latter showing significantly reduced intrinsic metabolic 
clearance for thiocyanate formation (Billaut-Laden et al., 2006a). 
TST polymorphisms that decrease cyanide and hydrogen sulfide 
detoxication may ultimately become important in understanding 
certain diseases, such as ulcerative colitis and ALS. For instance, 
hydrogen sulfide release has been found to be three to four times 
higher in patients with ulcerative colitis when compared with 
healthy subjects, and is positively correlated with disease severity 
(Billaut-Laden et al., 2006a). Furthermore, it has been postulated 
that individuals with ALS display a disorder in cyanide metabolism 
because of the higher blood and urine levels of cyanide (Billaut-
Laden et al., 2006a). Mimori et al. (1984) significantly decreased 
TST activity in cervical and thoracic spinal cords of patients with 
ALS compared with healthy subjects. Finally, TST has also been 
proposed as a target for the treatment of obesity and type 2 diabetes 
based on the observed inverse relationship between TST expression 
in adipose tissue and diabetes in rodents (Morton et al., 2016).
3-Mercaptopyruvate Sulfurtransferase
An additional pathway of cyanide detoxication involves the enzyme 
3-mercaptopyruvate sulfurtransferase (encoded by the gene MPST) 
that converts cyanide to thiocyanate by the transfer of sulfur from 
3-mercaptopyruvate as follows:

The 3-mercaptopyruvate in this reaction is produced during 
cysteine metabolism by cysteine aminotransferase (Billaut-Laden 
et al., 2006b; Nagahara et al., 2015). MPST is both a cytosolic and 
mitochondrial enzyme and is expressed at high levels in the kidney, 
liver, heart, and erythrocytes (Billaut-Laden et al., 2006b).
Three allelic variants of MPST have been identified, namely, 
MPST*1B, MPST*1C, and MPST*2, with the latter showing little-
to-no activity because it is a nonsense allele (Billaut-Laden et al., 
2006b). While TST and MPST are related enzymes due to their

374
Disposition of toxicants
unit II
shared amino acid sequence identity and function in cyanide detoxi-
fication, MPST enzymatic activity has been shown to be induced 
specifically by diallyl disulfide, suggesting a separate mechanism 
of regulation for each enzyme (Nakajima, 2015).
Unusual Conjugation Reactions
In rare cases, xenobiotics can be biotransformed to unusual con-
jugates which can contribute to xenobiotic clearance. As shown 
in Fig. 6-74, the immunosuppressive drug 6-mercaptopurine (as 
well as its precursor azathioprine) is converted to 6-thioinosine 
monophosphate (TIMP) by hypoxanthine guanine phophoribosyl-
transferase (HGPRT) with phophoribosyl pyrophosphate (PRPP) 
as the phophoribosyl donor (Wielinga et al., 2002; Gearry et al., 
2010; Hofmann et al., 2012). Similarly, to 6-mercaptopurine, 
6-thiopurine can also be converted to a thionucleotide mono-
phosphate (TNMP) (Wielinga et al., 2002). While the 6-thiogua-
nine nucleotides are responsible for immunosuppressant activity, 
6-methylmercaptopurine nucleotides have been associated with 
hepatotoxicity at high doses (Gearry et al., 2010).
Another example of an unusual conjugate is that of the immu-
nosuppressant macrolide everolimus, which forms a phosphocho-
line conjugate as a major metabolite (ATG181) in both animals and 
humans (Zollinger et al., 2008). While phosphocholine esters of 
endogenous lipids, such as sphingomyelins or phosphatidylcholines, 
are common, phosphocholine conjugates of xenobiotics are rare. The 
addition of activated phosphocholine is catalyzed by the enzymes 
cholinephosphotransferase found in the Golgi apparatus as well as 
choline/ethanolaminephosphotransferase found in the endoplasmic 
reticulum and nuclear envelope; however, it is not known whether 
these enzymes also catalyze the phosphocholine conjugation of 
everolimus (Zollinger et al., 2008). Hepatitis C inhibitors contain-
ing a bicycle[1.1.1]pentane moiety are also conjugated with phos-
phocholine (Zhuo et al., 2016), whereas pimasertib, an inhibitor of 
MEK1/2 (the mitogen-activated protein kinase signaling pathway), 
is conjugated with phosphoethanolamine (Scheible et al., 2017).

Biotransformation of Xenobiotics
Chapter 6
375
The hydroxyl group or a nitrogen atom in azoheterocyclic 
xenobiotics can be conjugated with ADP-ribose, the ADP moiety 
of which can be hydrolyzed by phosphatases to convert the ADP-
ribose conjugate to a ribose conjugate (Fan et al., 2016). The ADP-
ribosyltransferases (ART) responsible for the ADP-ribosylation 
of xenobiotics is known variously as NAD+ hydrolase, NAD-
glycohydrolase, nucleosidase, or NADase (EC 3.2.2.5), which use 
various nicotiminade cofactors including NAD, NADP, and/or NMN. 
Examples of substrates that undergo ADP-ribosylation are rifampin, 
imatinib, otenabant, continine, nicotinic acid, 4-ipomeanol, and nitro-
samines such as 4-(methylnitrosamino)-1-(3-pyridyl)-1-butanone 
(NNK) and 4-(methylnitrosamino)-1-(3-pyridyl)-1-butanol (NNAL). 
Different forms of ART are thought to be responsible for ADP-
ribosylation of xenobiotics in liver cytosol and endoplasmic reticulum 
(Fan et al., 2016). ADP-ribosylation is highly species specific and 
is mainly observed in rodents (i.e., imatinib, ipomeanol, and NNK/
NNAL) but not in dogs, monkeys, or humans (Fan et al., 2016).
While single amino-acid conjugation is a well characterized 
metabolic pathway for xenobiotic metabolism (see the section 
“Amino Acid Conjugation”), xenobiotic conjugation to a peptide 
moiety other than glutathione is rare. The metabolism of lucanthone 
is an example of this unusual peptide conjugation. In human liver 
microsomes and in incubations with fungi, lucanthone undergoes 
desethylation or 4-methyl hydroxylation to yield desethyl lucan-
thone and hycanthone, respectively, and these products can be 
further metabolized (Mitchell et al., 2014). Lucanthone can also 
undergo sulfoxidation to yield lucanthone sulfoxide, the major 
urinary metabolite excreted by monkey. Lucanthone sulfoxide is 
conjugated with a peptide moiety, named miracil X, which was 
shown to be a unique human urinary metabolite (Mitchell et al., 
2014). There are several other examples of peptide conjugation to 
xenobiotics, namely 3-phenoxybenzoic acid conjugation to dipep-
tide glycylvaline, quinaldic acid conjugates with glycylglycine 
and glycyltaurine, polyglutamyl conjugates of methotrexate and 
aminopterin, CCNU ((2-chloroethyl)-3-cyclohexyl-1-nitrosourea) 
peptide conjugate, DDA (4,4′-dichlorodiphenylacetic acid) peptide 
conjugate, and polypeptide conjugates of phenothiazine (Mitchell 
et al., 2014). However, the enzymatic pathways involved and the 
significance of these conjugates are not well established.
Succinylation has been shown to be a posttranslational modifi-
cation of proteins (typically at lysine residues); however, succinyl-
ation of xenobiotics is rare (Zhang et al., 2011b). The only reported 
case of this conjugation reaction is that of mephedrone, which is 
succinylated in humans. In studies with human volunteers dosed 
with mephedrone, a novel mephedrone succinate conjugate was 
detected in urine, and proposed to form from the conjugation of 
succinic acid to nor-mephedrone (Pozo et al., 2015). Succinylation 
of xenobiotics is typically used as a prodrug strategy to produce 
drugs with favorable properties; however, succinylation as a mecha-
nism of xenobiotic clearance is highly unusual (Pozo et al., 2015).
Another unusual conjugate is that of investigational antican-
cer agent β-lapachone, which forms a glucosylsulfate conjugate in 
mammals (Savage et al., 2008). Glucosidation and sulfonation of 
xenobiotics can readily occur (see the sections “Glucuronidation 
and Formation of Acyl-CoA Thioesters” and “Sulfonation”) by 
UGT and SULT enzymes. However, typically these Phase 2 reac-
tions are independent of each other and are not sequential to each 
other at the same metabolic site. In the case of β-lapachone, reduc-
tion by NOQ to a hydroquinone is followed by glucosidation, which 
in turn is followed by sulfonation of the glucose moiety (suspected 
to occur at glucose C6) forming the unusual glucosylsulfate con-
jugate (Savage et al., 2008). Glucosylsulfate conjugates have 
previously been observed in cotton plants treated with the insec-
ticide profenofos and 2,4-dicholorphenoxyacetic acid; in sugar 
beets treated with phenmedipham, and in crustaceans exposed to 
1-hydroxypyrene (Savage et al., 2008).
Lastly, the mechanism of xenobiotic conjugation to urea has 
been an area of some debate in the literature. These unusual con-
jugates have largely been considered as artifacts formed in urine 
through a nonenzymatic chemical reaction; however, there are 
reports of xenobiotic-urea metabolites found in bile and feces. An 
example of this is that of rosaramicin, where its urea metabolite 
20-bisureidorosaramicin was found in dog bile and feces, as well 
as human feces, suggesting an enzymatic process in the formation 
of this metabolite (Mitchell, 2014). Xenobiotics that form urea 
conjugates include aminopyrine, formaldehyde, oxazepam, carox-
azone, laropiprant, torcetrapib, isoproturon, and andrographolide 
(Mitchell, 2014). The significance of these conjugates and their 
mechanism of formation (chemical vs. metabolic) remain unclear.
Phosphorylation
Some drugs are intentionally designed to be phosphorylated by intra-
cellular enzymes. For example, the anti-HIV drug zidovudine (AZT) 
is converted to a triphosphate nucleoside by enzymes in the salvage 
pathway (nucleoside kinase, nucleoside monophosphate kinase 
[NMK], and nucleoside diphosphate kinase [NDK]). A rare exam-
ple of a non-nucleoside mimetic that undergoes phosphorylation is 
AZ2381, a tankyrase inhibitor, which is phosphorylated at its primary 
alcohol moiety by red blood cells from mice and, to a lesser extent, 
rat, dog, and human (Gu et al., 2017). However, phosphorylation 
of xenobiotics is the exception rather than the rule. All conjugation 
reactions ultimately require ATP, either to activate the xenobiotic for 
conjugation with GSH or amino acids or to synthesize high-energy 
cofactors such as UDPGA and PAPS. The process is inefficient in 
that several ATP molecules (or their equivalent) are used to synthe-
size each cofactor molecule. The question arises: why is ATP not 
used directly by conjugating enzymes? In other words, why are xeno-
biotics rarely phosphorylated directly (with the exception of nucleo-
side mimetics like zidovudine and the tankyrase inhinitor AZ2381), 
which would require less ATP and would achieve the goal of con-
verting xenobiotics to water-soluble conjugates? It is difficult to be 
certain why this does not occur, but four reasons suggest themselves. 
First, if xenobiotics could be phosphorylated, high intracellular levels 
of a xenobiotic might consume so much ATP as to jeopardize cell 
viability, whereas UDPGA and PAPS can be depleted without kill-
ing cells. Second, phosphorylation of endogenous substrates, such as 
glucose, is a mechanism for trapping endogenous substrates inside a 
cell. This works because the plasma membrane of all cells is a bar-
rier to the passage of polar compounds by virtue of its hydropho-
bic properties (lipid bilayer) and its general lack of transporters that 
efflux phosphorylated compounds out of the cell. The same is true 
for the phosphorylated metabolites of zidovudine and related anti-
viral drugs; they are retained inside the cell that forms them. A lipid 
bilayer is also a physical barrier to other water-soluble conjugates, 
such as glucuronides and sulfonates, but these are transported out of 
the cell by various transporters. Third, phosphorylation of both small 
molecules (such as inositol) and proteins (such as membrane-bound 
receptors and various transcription factors) plays an important role in 
intracellular and intranuclear signaling. It is possible that some xeno-
biotics, if they were phosphorylated, might interfere with these regu-
latory systems and thereby disrupt cellular homeostasis. Fourth, even 
if xenobiotics were phosphorylated in the liver and excreted in bile, 
they would be rapidly hydrolyzed by high levels of alkaline phos-
phatase lining the surface of enterocytes in the small intestine, which 
would promote reabsorption of the xenobiotic (by the very process 
exploited in the design of phosphorylated prodrugs such as fosampre-
navir). Whatever the reason, there appears to be strong evolutionary 
pressure against the conjugation of xenobiotics with phosphoric acid.

Disposition of toxicants
unit II
